author: @Sufyanlima
text:
The rule actually refers to the "second-last defender," because the goalkeeper is almost always behind the play.

On this play, though, Egypt's goalie had come out of his net, and he's no longer the last defender.

So the defender behind the ball more or less becomes like the goalie for the interpretation of this rule.

And because there wasn't a second player there, it's offside.

author: @Peaceeverwhere
text:
You could sympathize with iran team if you want, but their disallowed goal was an offside. 
If you do not know that, then educate yourself about football rules.

Law 11
A player is in an offside position if he is nearer to the opponents' goal line than both the ball and the second-last opponent.

Egypt goalkeeper was already in front of iran player, so iran player was nearer to the goal line than both the ball and the second last opponent (which is Egypt player number 9).

author: @SamralGuluzadeh
text:
Experts Criticizing the Decision and Considering It Unfair

On the other hand, some influential football commentators, and especially experts from the Middle East media, objected to the spirit of the decision:

The "Spirit of Football" Argument: This group of experts argued that the cancellation of such microscopic offsides, which do not give the attacker any significant advantage and are not noticeable to the eye, undermines the beauty and drama of football.

The Limits of VAR Intervention: Some analysts argued that the episode did not fall into the category of "clear and obvious error", and that VAR "stole" such a historic goal and qualification from Iran by scrutinizing the game in a situation where the linesman did not raise the flag.

author: @Whalesrooftop
text: When I was a kid, I played Pro Evolution Soccer on the PS. My habit was to first pass the ball short to my own player after the kickoff, then immediately follow up with a long kick or a long ball into the opponent's half. This kickoff style was rarely seen in real soccer matches. Even in the game back then, you had to wait for the second kick before you could launch a long ball; the first kick was always a forced short pass to your own player. I never imagined that nowadays, soccer kickoff tactics often involve launching a long ball directly to the opponent's goal with the first kick.😅

author: @jurist8140
text:
Until 1998, Japan couldn't even qualify for the World Cup, and they didn't even have a professional league until 1993. Since then, they’ve become regulars at the tournament. At first, their squad was entirely domestic-based, but gradually, players started making their mark in European clubs. Now, almost every member of the national team plays abroad.
In the last World Cup, they beat Germany and Spain, and they’ve officially declared their goal to win the World Cup by 2050—and they are steadily executing that plan. They even beat Brazil and England in pre-World Cup friendlies, and those were truly well-deserved wins.
Even with three of their key players—Mitoma, Endo, and Minamino—out with injuries, they’re putting on this kind of performance against the Netherlands. Just imagining what they could do with a full squad is beyond exciting. I can’t help but root for them!

author: @코렐-i5z
text:
10년 전에는 한국이 아시아 최강이었지만, 지금은 일본이 단연 아시아 최강이라는 사실을 인정해야 합니다.
